tEn: Mark Pincus on Proven Better New: A Framework for Successful Product Ideas
tZh: 马克·平卡斯谈“已验证更好新”：成功产品创意的框架
sEn: Mark Pincus shares his counterintuitive framework for coming up with successful product ideas, explaining why your instincts are right 95% of the time but your ideas are wrong 75% of the time.
sZh: 马克·平卡斯分享了他反直觉的产品创意框架，解释了为什么你的直觉 95%正确，但你的想法 75%错误。

consensus.en: Your instincts are right 95% of the time, but your ideas are wrong 75% of the time.
consensus.zh: 你的直觉 95%的时候是对的，但你的想法 75%的时候是错的。

consensus.en: Use the 'Proven, Better, New' framework: master proven elements, add small betterments, then test novel ideas.
consensus.zh: 使用“已验证、更好、创新”框架：掌握已验证元素，添加微小改进，然后测试新想法。

consensus.en: Kill hope before hope kills you; be intellectually honest when a product is a B+.
consensus.zh: 在希望杀死你之前扼杀希望；当产品是 B+时要保持智力诚实。

consensus.en: The best products start embarrassingly small and humble, not with grand ambition.
consensus.zh: 最好的产品始于微不足道和谦逊，而非宏大抱负。

consensus.en: Retention, especially day-365 retention, is the core metric for durable consumer products.
consensus.zh: 留存率，尤其是第 365 天留存率，是持久消费产品的核心指标。

consensus.en: Distribution must be baked into the product strategy from the start, not an afterthought.
consensus.zh: 分发必须从一开始就融入产品策略，而非事后考虑。

contrarian.en: Copying is a moral arbitrage; true innovation often starts by mastering and improving existing proven ideas.
contrarian.zh: 复制是一种道德套利；真正的创新通常始于掌握并改进已有的已验证想法。

contrarian.en: Being less ambitious gives startups an advantage over incumbents who need billion-dollar opportunities.
contrarian.zh: 不那么雄心勃勃让初创公司比需要十亿美元机会的现有企业更具优势。

contrarian.en: AI is not yet a new platform; we are still in the mobile and web era for consumer distribution.
contrarian.zh: AI 还不是一个新平台；在消费者分发方面，我们仍处于移动和网络时代。

contrarian.en: Micromanagement is beautiful; stay close to the metal and be in the room as long as possible.
contrarian.zh: 微观管理是美好的；尽可能贴近细节并亲临现场。

contrarian.en: The CEO's number one job is to be right, even over execution or inspiration.
contrarian.zh: CEO 的首要任务是做对，即使胜过执行或激励。

contrarian.en: Social media has lost its adrenaline; people are proud to quit Instagram, creating latent demand for reinvention.
contrarian.zh: 社交媒体已失去肾上腺素；人们以退出 Instagram 为荣，这为重塑创造了潜在需求。

turns.en: If you're truly ambitious, burn your resume. You have all these amazing contrarian perspectives on how to build amazing products. Your instincts are right 95% of the time. Your ideas are wrong 75% of the time. We've seen so many founders who just stoically, heroically stick with a losing idea. How do you know if this is just the wrong path you're following? If you're asking whether or not your product is an A, it's not an A. When you have lightning in a bottle, when you have true signal, everything works. Most products are better versions of things that existed before. Talk about how you get over that hump of copying. It's almost a moral arbitrage. You became a founder, an entrepreneur because you wanted to go be an innovator, but you're trying to win the hearts and minds of nurses in Indiana like for Farmville. You're not trying to win awards and respect from your peers. Define your ambition in the eyes of your consumer. Building a consumer social app, very few people have successfully done it and built something durable. We have beyond a latent demand for social. It's lost the adrenaline. People are proud to tell you they're not on Instagram. They're not missing the party. If you want to reinvent social, look for where the cocktail is. We know it when we see a great cocktail party. You feel you're like, 'Oh, I'm so glad I'm here.' Today, we're all hanging out on our Claude on our GPT, but there's no cocktail party. My challenge to your listeners is figure out how to make it rowdy. Today my guest is Mark Pincus, founder of Zynga, who has arguably created more successful consumer products than anyone else in history, over a dozen both within Zynga and before Zynga. And over the past 5 years, he has been working on a book that synthesizes all of the things that he's learned about building successful consumer products. It's called Life at the Speed of Play. It's coming out in a few weeks, and it is so good, and it's also a really quick read. In a quote for the book, Sam Altman, the co-founder of OpenAI, said that today the only bottleneck to building great products is knowing what to create. Mark is an expert at this. And after reading this book and having this conversation, I could not agree more. In his book and in this conversation, Mark shares a really clever and counterintuitive framework that he's developed for coming up with successful product ideas. Why being less ambitious is often the path to coming up with the most ambitious ideas. Why you need to kill your hope before your hope kills you. Why your instincts are usually right, but your ideas are usually wrong. Also, what he's learned about raising kids. He's got five. And so, so, so much more. This episode is for anybody who is building a product or thinking about starting a company. Before we get into it, don't forget to check out lennisprod.com for a free year of the hottest and most well-crafted AI products in the world, available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers. With that, I bring you Mark Pincus.

turns.en: Thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast. I've been a big listener. Oh, wow. And I'm excited to finally be on.
turns.zh: 非常感谢你来到这里，欢迎来到播客。我一直是你的忠实听众。哦，哇。我很兴奋终于能来了。

turns.en: I'm excited to finally have you on. You just put out a book. It's coming out around the same time that this podcast is coming out. It's called Life at the Speed of Play. There's so much here I want to talk about. I want to start with this framework that you developed called Proven Better New, and the framework is basically designed to help you come up with and refine your product ideas and your startup ideas to basically increase the odds that your idea is a good idea and that it will work. It is such a clever idea, such a simple idea with so much depth. I want to spend a bunch of time on this. So first of all, just give us kind of the overview on this framework, what it is, how people might use it in coming up with ideas.
turns.zh: 我很兴奋终于请到你了。你刚出了一本书，和这期播客差不多同时发布，书名是《Life at the Speed of Play》。我想聊的太多了。我想从你开发的这个框架开始，叫做‘Proven Better New’，这个框架基本上是为了帮助你产生和完善产品创意以及创业想法，从而增加你的想法是好主意并且能成功的概率。这是一个非常巧妙的想法，简单却深刻。我想花些时间讨论这个。所以首先，给我们一个这个框架的概述，它是什么，人们如何用它来产生想法。

turns.en: Sure. Well, this is a framework that we got to early on at Zynga and it became like a religion and the fundamental core principles and engine behind how we did product management at Zynga. And it was so fundamental to the book that for three of the four years writing the book we called it Proven Better New but then I was like oh that's too dry a name and I love this concept of Life at the Speed of Play which we can talk about but is the bigger gestalt to the book. So, but Proven Better New is this core philosophy that I've had around products since I started Zynga, which is that we have these instincts like in our gut, these human level instincts that are pretty much always right. And then we put these ideas on top of the instincts that are usually wrong. And my rule of thumb is your instincts are right 95% of the time. Your ideas are wrong 75% or at best right 25% of the time. The framework of Proven Better New takes that philosophy and says okay what do we do with that? Okay let's isolate your innovation zone. Let's isolate that thing you have in your gut and let's just test many many ideas around that and let's fail for the right reason not the wrong reason. It might be I mean at Zynga we'd see new game launches. Sid Meier's like the you know godfather of game design who's the most revered game designer. He came out, I think, a social civilization on Facebook and we thought, 'Oh god, here comes like the ultimate game designer.' And 10 minutes after the game came out, the Zynga PMs emailed around their analysis and they said, 'It's dead on arrival because his first time user experience was so many clicks and so bad that no one was ever going to see his great game design.' Even Sid Meier tripped over what were understood by the most junior product managers at Zynga was the best of breed approach to onboarding a new user to the first-time user experience on the Facebook platform. But because he didn't perfectly copy that, he didn't do the proven right, his innovation never got seen by anybody.
turns.zh: 当然。这个框架我们在 Zynga 早期就采用了，它几乎成了我们的信仰，是 Zynga 产品管理的核心原则和引擎。它对这本书如此重要，以至于在写书的四年中有三年我们都把书叫做《Proven Better New》，但后来我觉得这个名字太干巴巴了，我喜欢‘Life at the Speed of Play’这个概念，我们可以聊聊，这是书的大框架。但‘Proven Better New’是我从创办 Zynga 以来一直秉持的产品核心理念，那就是我们都有直觉，这些人类层面的直觉几乎总是对的。然后我们把想法加在直觉之上，这些想法通常是错的。我的经验法则是：你的直觉 95%是对的，你的想法 75%是错的，最多 25%是对的。‘Proven Better New’框架就是基于这个理念，然后说：好吧，我们该怎么办？我们来隔离你的创新区域。隔离你直觉中的那个东西，然后围绕它测试很多很多想法，让我们为正确的理由失败，而不是错误的理由。比如在 Zynga，我们看到新游戏发布。Sid Meier，你知道，游戏设计教父，最受尊敬的游戏设计师。他在 Facebook 上推出了一款社交文明游戏，我们想，‘哦天哪，终极游戏设计师来了。’游戏发布 10 分钟后，Zynga 的产品经理就发邮件分析说：‘它一上线就死了，因为他的首次用户体验点击太多、太糟糕，没人会看到他的伟大游戏设计。’就连 Sid Meier 也栽在了 Zynga 最初级的产品经理都理解的、在 Facebook 平台上引导新用户的最佳实践上。但因为他没有完美复制那个，没有做‘已被证明正确’的事，他的创新就没人看到。

turns.en: And so the point of proven better new is to say let's take all the proven off the table. If you want to build the AI Snapchat or the AI camera, there's so many of those I see now, AI cameras. Fine. Let's start with what you're not innovating on. You're the icon. Maybe just the way a camera works. Look for where that's the best of breed proven, whether it's Apple or Snapchat or Instagram, and copy those legally and with hopefully some taste. We can get into like great copies and bad copies, but be a master of the proven first. Get your PhD in proven first. And I like to say we don't we haven't earned the right to innovate on the camera until we are the world's leading PhD on the best mobile cameras that already exist.
turns.zh: 所以‘成熟、更好、创新’的重点是，先把所有成熟的东西摆到台面上。如果你想打造 AI Snapchat 或 AI 相机——我现在看到很多这样的 AI 相机——没问题。先从你不创新的部分开始。比如图标，或者相机的工作方式。找到那些已经证明是最佳实践的地方，无论是苹果、Snapchat 还是 Instagram，合法地复制它们，最好还有点品味。我们可以讨论好的复制和坏的复制，但首先要成为成熟领域的专家。先拿到成熟领域的博士学位。我想说的是，在我们成为现有最佳移动相机的世界级专家之前，我们还没有资格在相机上创新。

turns.en: And then better is usually we can't find better. Better is usually very small increments and innovations. And better is something that 10 out of 10 of your existing users of that product would say yeah. 10 out of 10 not you. What you think is better is called new, right? What we think is better is new. We usually are too ambitious on the new and so better could be it's now free. There's no download. There's something that you can both statistically show works, you know, is better and every user would say yes to. And usually it's very small and it's polish and it's things that intense users, power users will notice the most.
turns.zh: 而‘更好’通常是我们找不到的。‘更好’往往是非常小的增量改进和创新。‘更好’是那种现有用户中十个人里有十个都会说‘是的，这更好’的东西。是十个人里十个，不是你。你认为的‘更好’其实叫‘创新’，对吧？我们以为的‘更好’其实是‘创新’。我们通常对‘创新’太雄心勃勃了。所以‘更好’可以是：现在免费了、无需下载、你能用数据证明它更好、而且每个用户都会同意。通常它非常微小，是打磨，是那些重度用户、核心用户最能注意到的细节。

turns.en: And in our game, Words with Friends, you know, it was Scrabble, but if it was just Scrabble, why was it a massive hit with 14 million DAUs and Scrabble itself wasn't right? There must have been something, you know, better and novel about it. But it had such a polish for mobile was the better and the new. What's the novel new idea, the back of the box idea that's going to get people to download and try it? And in the case of Words with Friends, it was social. It was your friends are just attached to the Facebook graph and your friends are just already there to play with you, which was a new idea. You know, even that which you'd think of course I want, you might not get 10 out of 10 people saying yes. And we have to accept that that new idea is probably going to fail. It's a reason to try it for people, but it's probably going to fail.
turns.zh: 以我们的游戏《Words with Friends》为例，它本质上是 Scrabble，但如果只是 Scrabble，为什么它能大获成功，拥有 1400 万日活用户，而 Scrabble 本身却没有？一定有什么‘更好’和‘新颖’的地方。它对移动端的打磨就是‘更好’，而‘新颖’是什么？那个写在盒子背面的、能吸引人们下载尝试的新点子是什么？在《Words with Friends》里，是社交。你的朋友通过 Facebook 图谱直接关联，他们就在那里等着和你一起玩——这是一个新点子。但即使是这样，你觉得‘我当然想要’，可能十个人里也不会有十个人说‘是’。我们必须接受，那个新点子很可能会失败。它是人们尝试的理由，但很可能失败。

turns.en: And if we do proven better new right, the product has much better odds of succeeding in the market, not failing for the wrong reasons. And if we start with the premise that the new is probably not right, and we've got four other new ideas ready to test, then we just prosecute it in a different way. And we really it's I like to think if you do proven better new right? It's like a time machine because what if I could go back to Mark, you know, 2003 and four and say, 'Dude, you don't even realize how right you are.' Like, one of your instincts is going to be a $1.6 trillion company once, and you are a year ahead of them, right? You're a year ahead of a $1.6 trillion company. That's, you know, I'm not going to tell you which. Oh, by the way, your Tribes idea that's Reddit that becomes a whole company industry, you know, listings.
turns.zh: 如果我们正确运用‘成熟、更好、创新’，产品在市场上成功的几率会大得多，不会因为错误的原因失败。如果我们一开始就假设‘创新’很可能不对，并且我们还有另外四个新点子准备测试，那么我们就会以不同的方式推进。我真的觉得，如果你正确运用‘成熟、更好、创新’，它就像一台时间机器。因为如果我能回到 2003 或 2004 年，对马克说：‘老兄，你都不知道自己有多正确。’比如，你的一种直觉将来会成就一家 1.6 万亿美元的公司，而你比他们领先一年，对吧？你比一家 1.6 万亿美元的公司领先一年。我不会告诉你是哪家，我不会完全作弊，但我只想告诉你，这里面还有另一个答案，你可以追求，价值 1.6 万亿美元。哦，顺便说一句，你的 Tribes 想法就是 Reddit，它后来成了一个完整的公司行业，你知道的，列表。

turns.en: So, there was a point in Tribe where I knew my metrics weren't working. Our D30 retention was terrible. We were sinking speedboat. And if I had had this attitude of I'm going to try a lot more ideas. I'm going to look around me like if you're doing proven better, you're also looking around you for what's what are you finding heat that's proven in the market that you can also go test and you know I would have massively changed my odds of success.
turns.zh: 所以，在 Tribe 的某个时刻，我知道我的指标不行了。我们的 30 天留存率很糟糕，我们就像一艘正在下沉的快艇。如果我当时有这种态度——我要尝试更多点子，我要环顾四周——因为当你做‘成熟、更好、创新’时，你也会环顾四周，寻找市场上已经验证的热点，然后去测试——那么我成功的几率会大大改变。

turns.zh: 好的，这里信息量很大。我来总结一下我对这个框架的理解，它非常棒。如果你仔细想想所有成功的产品，有多少实际上遵循了这个框架——无论是有意还是无意——这很惊人。我听到的步骤是：成熟——列出市场上已经验证有效的东西，人们喜欢这个领域的哪些方面；更好——做出不仅更好，而且十个人里有十个都会说‘妈的，我会换这个，这更好’的东西；然后加上一些没人尝试过的新东西，增加一点变化。我觉得你在书里有很多例子，比如 Slack 和 Threads。还有，如果你想想 iPhone 和 iPod，你可能不会那样想它们，但基本上 iPod 之前有音乐播放器，他们把它做得更好，并添加了一些新东西。

transcript.sec: Copying as a starting point for innovation

turns.en: So if you take a behavior they like but you make it much more accessible or something much more fun about it like in the case of Slack, people love that even more. There's this thread of copying that comes through this framework that will turn a lot of people off. turns.zh: 所以，如果你采用他们喜欢的行为，但让它更容易获得或更有趣，就像 Slack 那样，人们会更喜欢。这个框架中有一种“复制”的线索，会让很多人反感。谈谈你认为人们忽略了什么，以及你如何克服这种“这实际上在大多数情况下是正确的做法”的心理障碍。

turns.en: In the Peter Thiel sense, it's almost a moral arbitrage because there's something in our gut as a product person. You became a founder, an entrepreneur because you wanted to go be an innovator, and so it can feel like a beatdown that your path to innovation starts with copying other people's work. And we were taught in school copying is cheating. So there's a lot of good reasons why we've built this kind of moral resistance to copying. But that also makes that opportunity in some ways more available for people who have less ego involved. And I like to say, and I said this to my product makers at Zynga, if you're truly ambitious, burn your resume. If you define your ambition in the eyes of your consumer, not your peers, you're not trying to win awards and respect from your peers. You're trying to win the hearts and minds of nurses in Indiana like for FarmVille. You're going to define innovation differently and you're not going to worry about whether you're going to take the best ideas wherever you can find them if they are in service of giving her an experience that she loves more. So if all you did was copy, there's no reason for her to choose your product, right? But if you took something that she loves and you make it one inch better, she might love that more than if you showed her something she's never seen before and didn't wake up knowing that she wanted. And I think the art of this is you do it in a way that she doesn't even realize it because I think consumer taste also resists just a copy. If movies, TV shows, books, if they feel too derivative and they're not adding something important and new, then it could go against consumer taste. But the best product makers, and I love referencing someone like Craig Newmark on Craigslist, who took two years to add photos to Craigslist listings. And I was like, and he lived in my neighborhood, and he was friends with my dog, Zynga, more than he was friends with me. He was a little awkward socially, and he connected more with the dog. And I tried to be friends with him through the dog. And so he would sit and talk to me and he was working on I was like, "What are you working?" And I said, "I've been working for two years on adding photos to listings." I'm like, "What do you mean two years?" He's like, "Well, I really want to make sure that people like it and that the photos come up in the right ways. I don't know. I honestly don't even understand what he did for two years, but if you're driving across San Francisco to buy someone's couch, why wouldn't you want to? Why isn't that better? In his mind, it was new." And I'm like, that's a world-class product maker because he gets that we feel this sense of ownership of products that we rely on every day. And we're angry when they change. Even if they change for the better, we're angry because we go through life faster because of pattern recognition. And we don't want to think about these things. And what if by adding photos to his listings, he moved where the text was? And what if you rifle through listings and now the text is below the fold, you know, and a junior product maker might make the whole thing a picture and not realize that what people liked most was being able to see the text real quick and they were trying to compare prices on the couch they already knew that I don't know.
turns.zh: 从彼得·蒂尔的角度看，这几乎是一种道德套利，因为作为产品人，我们内心深处有一种东西。你成为创始人、企业家，是因为你想成为创新者，所以创新之路从复制别人的作品开始，感觉像是一种打击。而且我们在学校被教导复制就是作弊。所以我们有很多理由对复制产生道德抵触。但这也在某种程度上让那些不那么自我的人更容易获得机会。我喜欢说，而且我对 Zynga 的产品经理说过，如果你真的雄心勃勃，就烧掉你的简历。如果你从消费者的角度而非同行的角度定义你的雄心，你不是为了赢得奖项和同行的尊重。你是在努力赢得印第安纳州护士们的心，就像 FarmVille 那样。你会以不同的方式定义创新，你不会担心是否要借鉴最好的想法，只要它们能服务于给她一个她更喜欢的体验。所以，如果你只是复制，她没有理由选择你的产品，对吧？但如果你拿了她喜欢的东西，并让它好了一点点，她可能会更喜欢，而不是你给她展示一个她从未见过、醒来时也没意识到自己想要的东西。我认为这门艺术在于你以一种她甚至没有意识到的方式去做，因为我认为消费者的品味也抵制单纯的复制。如果电影、电视节目、书籍感觉太衍生，没有增加重要和新颖的东西，那就会违背消费者的品味。但最好的产品制造者，我喜欢引用 Craigslist 的 Craig Newmark，他花了两年时间在 Craigslist 列表中添加照片。我当时想，他住在我附近，他和我的狗 Zynga 比和我更亲近。他在社交上有点笨拙，和狗更亲近。我试图通过狗和他交朋友。所以他坐下来和我聊天，他在工作，我问：“你在做什么？”他说：“我花了两年时间在列表中添加照片。”我说：“两年是什么意思？”他说：“嗯，我真的想确保人们喜欢它，并且照片以正确的方式出现。我不知道。老实说，我甚至不明白他两年做了什么，但如果你开车穿过旧金山去买别人的沙发，你为什么不想看照片？那不是更好吗？在他看来，这是新的。”我说，这就是世界级的产品制造者，因为他明白我们对每天依赖的产品有一种所有权感。当它们改变时，我们会生气。即使改变是好的，我们也会生气，因为我们通过模式识别更快地生活。我们不想思考这些事情。如果通过添加照片，他移动了文本的位置呢？如果你浏览列表，现在文本在折叠线以下呢？一个初级产品经理可能会把整个东西做成图片，而没有意识到人们最喜欢的是能够快速看到文本，他们试图比较他们已经知道的沙发的价格。我不知道。

turns.en: Well, there's so much on this that we could literally spend the whole podcast on it, which I'd be fine with, too, or do a whole episode just on this because so much comes up for me. turns.zh: 嗯，关于这一点有很多可说的，我们完全可以花整个播客来讨论，我也没问题，或者专门做一集，因为我有很多想法。一方面，我想到了 Nikita，我想你请过他，我喜欢他在 TBH 上的故事。这是一个完美的例子，他在别人的产品中找到了经过验证的东西。那是我第一家公司 Freeloader。我在 Netscape 浏览器和 Internet Explorer 浏览器中发现了一个隐藏的功能：离线浏览。我的整个产品就是离线浏览，但他们把它作为一个隐藏的高级用户功能。我看到了那个时刻的天才之处，因为带宽太慢了，我想，“哦，我们可以围绕这一个功能做一个完整的产品。”Nikita 看到了他完美的产品隐藏在他产品的阿拉伯语版本中，他说，“天哪，他们做到了。”所以我认为，当我们能在别人的产品中看到已经验证的东西，但他们对此有错误的想法时，那就是金子。这是其一。然后回到你的问题，是否每个产品都是衍生的，每个产品都来自经过验证的更好东西，我的答案是否定的。我在书和课程中稍微谈到了这一点。但问题在于，一开始我们所有人都要选择走哪条路？我认为，如果你走纯粹创新的路，从一块空白白板开始，不看任何其他产品，那就像 Rovio 做出《愤怒的小鸟》那样。他们做了 45 款游戏，每一款都完全不同。

transcript.sec: Moral arbitrage and being less ambitious

turns.en: Not as far as I could tell learning from the previous failures or the market. Their 45th shot on goal was Angry Birds, and it was a hit and innovative. But the odds are like wildcat drilling. Do you want those odds, or do you want to be like OMG Pop? Similar story but opposite path. OMG Pop created the hit game Draw Something, and Zynga bought them. They were the number one game and app in the app store for 60 days in 2012. But OMG Pop was on their last dollar. They had tried something totally innovative, never seen before, and it failed completely. Now they were ruthlessly, desperately in need of a hit. So they did Proven Better Newly. They said, 'We're going to take this proven game and do everything about it that is already working and viral on mobile.' They perfectly copied our turn-based system from Words with Friends and made a massive hit.
turns.zh: 据我所知，他们并没有从之前的失败或市场中吸取教训。他们的第 45 次尝试是《愤怒的小鸟》，结果大获成功且富有创新。但那种几率就像野猫钻井一样。你想要那样的几率，还是想成为 OMG Pop 那样？故事类似但路径相反。OMG Pop 创造了热门游戏《Draw Something》，Zynga 收购了他们。2012 年，他们在应用商店连续 60 天排名第一。但 OMG Pop 当时已经山穷水尽。他们尝试了完全创新的东西，前所未有，结果彻底失败。现在他们急需一个爆款。所以他们采用了“已验证的更好新”策略。他们说：‘我们要借鉴这款已验证的游戏，把移动端已经成功和病毒式传播的元素全部用上。’他们完美复制了我们《Words with Friends》的回合制系统，做出了一个巨大的成功。

turns.en: I love this idea of moral arbitrage. That's such a way of thinking about this.
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个道德套利的概念。这真是一种思考方式。

turns.en: Peter Thiel, you know. Very. Yeah. So there are so many fun threads that connect to all this. One is you have all these amazing contrarian perspectives on how to build amazing products. One is be less ambitious. Talk about why that's a really good lens to actually build more ambitious products.
turns.zh: 彼得·蒂尔，你知道的。非常。是的。所以有很多有趣的线索与此相关。其中之一是你有所有这些关于如何打造出色产品的逆向观点。一个是“少一些野心”。谈谈为什么这实际上是构建更具野心产品的绝佳视角。

turns.en: I'm in the middle of relearning that lesson myself right now. And I would say there are so many paradoxes in this. One that I've had to learn over and over again is if we're too ambitious and visionary at the outset about the product we want to build, then we will probably miss product-market fit because we won't start at a small enough, humble enough place. It might usually be that the starting point for these products is embarrassingly small. So many of these massive hit products or franchises started with such humble, non-ambitious places. Facebook was just an app to check out girls and guys at Harvard, right? The irony for me is that my career has gone in these waves. I started in a very humble place of just wanting to get to any product-market fit. I had bigger success than I imagined in my first two companies, Freeloader and Support.com. They both started with very small, humble premises. Afterwards, I felt like now I can do anything. I know Elon is the exception to the rule. Part of it is he can raise magical unlimited amounts of capital, which helps, and he does not start in humble places at all. But for the rest of us mortals, after success we tend to feel the need to do something bigger next time. That's human instinct. We want to be more ambitious. So when I got to Tribe and saw social networking, LinkedIn was just starting, and I saw how big the opportunity was, I tried to do everything with Tribe. It was really ambitious, and I didn't pick one use case. There were multiple inside Tribe that worked. The idea of urban tribes worked. People loved it. But I was too ambitious, so we failed. Then I was so humbled and in this abyss for so long, so desperate to get out, that by the time I got to Zynga, I did something embarrassingly small. I mean, to be a 41-year-old multi-time successful founder who could go do something important in the world, what did I do? I made a Facebook app. Like a poker game. Not even a Facebook app. I mean a poker game. People thought I had no dignity. They were like, 'Mark, there's so much you could do in the world.' But my ambition came down to like a thousand-foot altitude, not 100,000-foot. And that was the key to success. I see this with so many product founders. It almost gives a new founder an advantage over a multi-time successful founder because we have too much rope to hang ourselves. It's too easy to raise money and recruit teams against a big vision before we've gotten to product-market fit. But the world doesn't care about your resume. That's the good news for everyone watching. Everyone has the same chance. In a lot of ways, if you're in a more humble place, you have a better opportunity. So the paradox is the more ambitious you are, the more humble you should be and the smaller place you should be willing to start.
turns.zh: 我现在正在重新学习这一课。我想说这里面有很多悖论。我反复学到的一点是，如果我们一开始对要构建的产品过于雄心勃勃、过于有远见，那么我们很可能会错过产品市场契合，因为我们不会从一个足够小、足够谦逊的地方开始。这些产品的起点通常小得令人尴尬。许多巨大的热门产品或系列都是从如此谦逊、毫无野心的起点开始的。Facebook 最初只是一个在哈佛看帅哥美女的应用，对吧？讽刺的是，我的职业生涯就是一波一波的。我从一个非常谦逊的起点开始，只想找到任何产品市场契合。我的前两家公司 Freeloader 和 Support.com 取得了超出想象的成功。它们都始于非常小、非常谦逊的前提。之后我觉得自己无所不能。我知道埃隆是例外。部分原因是他能筹集到神奇无限的资本，这有帮助，而且他根本不会从谦逊的地方开始。但对于我们这些凡人来说，成功之后我们往往觉得下次需要做更大的事。这是人的本能。我们想更有野心。所以当我创办 Tribe，看到社交网络，LinkedIn 刚刚起步，我看到了巨大的机会，我试图用 Tribe 做所有事。那真的很有野心，我没有选择一个用例。Tribe 内部有好几个功能都有效。城市部落的想法很成功。人们喜欢它。但我太有野心了，所以我们失败了。然后我深受打击，在深渊里待了很久，非常渴望出来，所以当我创办 Zynga 时，我做了一件小得令人尴尬的事。我的意思是，作为一个 41 岁、多次成功的创始人，本可以去做一些重要的事，我做了什么？我做了一个 Facebook 应用。一个扑克游戏。甚至不是 Facebook 应用。就是一个扑克游戏。人们觉得我没有尊严。他们说：‘马克，世界上有那么多你可以做的事。’但我的野心降到了 1000 英尺的高度，而不是 10 万英尺。这就是成功的关键。我在很多产品创始人身上都看到了这一点。这几乎让新创始人比多次成功的创始人更有优势，因为我们的绳子太长，容易把自己吊死。在找到产品市场契合之前，很容易凭借一个大愿景筹集资金和招募团队。但世界不在乎你的履历。这对所有观众来说是好消息。每个人都有同样的机会。在很多方面，如果你处于更谦逊的位置，你就有更好的机会。所以悖论是：你越有野心，就越应该谦逊，越应该愿意从更小的地方开始。

turns.en: One of your insights along these lines is that this is your advantage as a startup: to be less ambitious. Because Zuck needs to go really big since the revenue is already so high. Anything they take on needs to be a multi-billion dollar business opportunity. As a startup, you don't need to do that. And this is how you end up discovering some of the biggest ideas in the end.
turns.zh: 你在这方面的一个见解是，作为初创公司，你的优势就是少一些野心。因为扎克伯格需要做得非常大，因为收入已经很高了。他们做的任何事都需要是数十亿美元的商业机会。而作为初创公司，你不需要那样做。最终，这恰恰是你发现一些最伟大想法的方式。

turns.en: Yeah. Being willing to pursue these little threads when they're flaky, when they're not a business, when they're so much earlier. And so many of the successful companies today started there.
turns.zh: 是的。愿意去追求这些小小的线索，当它们还不成熟、还不是一门生意、还处于非常早期的时候。如今许多成功的公司都是从那里起步的。

turns.en: Yeah. Is there an example that comes to mind? What's a company or two that started very unambitious?
turns.zh: 是的。有没有什么例子？哪一两家公司是从非常没有野心的起点开始的？

turns.en: I love the story of Bolt.new that really blew up last year. I loved it so much I cold-emailed the founder because I was so impressed.
turns.zh: 我喜欢 Bolt.new 的故事，它去年真的火了。我非常喜欢，以至于我直接给创始人发了冷邮件，因为我印象太深刻了。

turns.en: Yeah. And they toiled in obscurity on something they were into, and then they open-sourced it. I think they were barely able to keep going with commercial development. Then they had this realization one day: wait a second, if we take these web stacks, this virtual machine that we've been making work on the web, but we actually add that to an AI coding copilot, we now have something that's better than what anybody else has. I think that was just a great story. They were passionate about one thing and stuck with it.
turns.zh: 是的。他们默默无闻地钻研自己感兴趣的东西，然后开源了。我觉得他们几乎无法继续商业开发了。然后有一天他们意识到：等等，如果我们把这些网络栈、这个我们一直在网上运行的虚拟机，实际上把它加入一个 AI 编程助手，我们现在就有了比别人更好的东西。我认为那真是一个很棒的故事。他们热爱一件事，并坚持了下来。

turns.en: Yeah, Eric was on the podcast. We talked about that.
turns.zh: 是的，Eric 上过播客。我们聊过这个。

turns.en: Oh, he was so impressed. In a lot of ways Slack also, you know, was humbled. Stewart keeps trying to start game companies, and those turn into unexpected bigger hit companies. We'll see what he does next. Now has probably so much capital he could just keep doing a game company this time.
turns.zh: 哦，他印象很深。在很多方面，Slack 也是，它曾经很谦逊。Stewart 一直试图创办游戏公司，结果却变成了意想不到的大热门公司。我们看看他接下来会做什么。现在他可能资本雄厚，这次可以继续做游戏公司了。

turns.en: But I think to your point, he might try again to unhumbled go big.
turns.zh: 但我想你说的对，他可能会再次尝试不再谦逊，去做大。

turns.en: Yeah. But each time, right? So he started a big idea for like the mass market MMO, which I've also loved but really difficult. And they were humbled in that enough to say, 'Okay, wait. There's this little teeny thing that our engineers use.'
turns.zh: 是的。但每次都是这样，对吧？所以他开始了一个面向大众市场的 MMO 的大想法，我也很喜欢，但真的很难。他们在这方面受挫，以至于说：‘好吧，等等。我们工程师用的这个小东西。’

transcript.secZh: 道德套利与降低野心

transcript.sec: Founder Mode: Killing Hope and Bad Ideas

turns.en: That's exactly where I wanted to go, which is this other piece of advice you give: kill hope before hope kills you. Talk about what that is, because so much of your advice is kill your bad ideas quickly. So just talk about that and then I'm really curious to know what it takes to realize an idea is bad. When do you decide, okay, this is B+, let's move on?
turns.zh: 这正是我想问的，就是你给出的另一条建议：在希望杀死你之前先扼杀希望。谈谈这是什么意思，因为你很多建议都是快速扼杀坏点子。所以先聊聊这个，然后我很好奇，要意识到一个想法是坏的需要什么条件？你什么时候决定，好吧，这是个 B+，我们继续前进？

turns.en: I don't know if I get credit for making up that quote, but I love it. Kill hope before hope kills you. There's a difference between belief and hope. Hope is confidence without basis. Hope is just a prayer, but it's not founded in anything from your lived experience, your experience with your product, people experiencing your product, and the data. I think too many founders and teams keep going with the hope that this next release will do something magical. I think the best product makers are collecting winnings, not making bets. They already know. If you talk to Brian Chesky about his launches, he already knows he has a hit. He's not launching it to find out if people like it. I talk about the difference between launching an MVP, a minimum viable product, and a maximum launchable product. Because if we're going off hope, we're getting to this MVP. 'Viable' is the word we need to kill along with hope, because viable is where hope comes from. If it's viable, it might make it. There's a difference between getting to market early to learn and launching a product you believe, not hope, will be a hit. It's important to be crisp and make that distinction. AI is a dangerous tool today. It's so powerful that it's dangerous. It means we can get to a viable product in much less time and money—maybe 3 months instead of 3 years. That's a dangerous drug because I thought we'd be using AI to build incredible testing machines, failure machines that test more ideas in a week than your industry tests in a year. How are you testing 100 ideas a day instead of one in three months? I think AI is being used more to build one idea in three months than a hundred ideas in a day. What would it look like? I like to say we should build it completely wrong before we know it's the right product. Build it wrong before you know it's right. If you can build it right, great, but don't be slowed down by that. If we start off saying, 'Okay, what can I believe today? I believe this is the wrong product.' What will you do differently? You won't waste three months building the wrong product. I'd rather waste a day or a week building the wrong product. The point is to learn. Is it good enough to get signal and test my idea? We can usually pull that apart and test those pieces in the wrong way. So often it could be an ad. I cannot believe how many times a team hasn't even tested the ads for their product before launching. It's an afterthought. At Zynga, it blew my mind. One story: when we launched our first expansion pack for FarmVille—which became massive business drivers—I think it was FarmVille English Countryside. The team came to me and said, 'We have a $10 million ad budget and we're going to start running ads before launch.' I said, 'Wait, you have 25-30 million people a day using your product, but you're going to put ads elsewhere for coming soon? You have these people engaged. What if you just put something on the game board? And what if you don't just start product marketing, but you're still doing product testing to see what gets the most heat? What if you start putting different art forms of your English countryside locked on the game board and just see how many clicks you get? Start testing the look, feel, marketing message. At the same time, when people click on it, it says 'Coming soon. Click here to be a pre-user. Click here to get access two weeks before anybody else.' It turned out everybody clicked on it, because it's a change on your game board. It gave us direction on the right marketing and the right variant of the product. But the unintended consequence was we started selling keys to people for you and a friend, and we ended up selling $19 million worth of keys to get early access to the new expansion pack.
turns.zh: 我不知道这句话是不是我原创的，但我很喜欢。在希望杀死你之前先扼杀希望。信念和希望是有区别的。希望是没有根据的信心。希望只是一种祈祷，但它不是基于你的亲身经历、你与产品的互动、用户使用产品的体验以及数据。我认为太多创始人和团队抱着希望继续前进，指望下一个版本能创造奇迹。我认为最好的产品制造者是在收集胜利，而不是下注。他们早就知道了。如果你和布莱恩·切斯基聊他的发布，他早就知道那会是个爆款。他不是通过发布来测试人们是否喜欢。我谈过发布 MVP（最小可行产品）和最大可发布产品之间的区别。因为如果我们依赖希望，我们就会走向 MVP。'可行'这个词是我们需要和希望一起扼杀的，因为希望就来自可行。如果它可行，它可能成功。尽早进入市场学习和发布一个你相信（而不是希望）会成功的产品是有区别的。保持清晰并做出这个区分很重要。AI 今天是一个危险的工具。它太强大了，以至于危险。这意味着我们可以用更少的时间和金钱得到一个可行产品——也许 3 个月而不是 3 年。这是一种危险的毒品，因为我原以为我们会用 AI 来构建令人难以置信的测试机器、失败机器，一周内测试的想法比你行业一年测试的还多。你怎么能一天测试 100 个想法而不是三个月测试一个？我认为 AI 更多地被用来在三个月内构建一个想法，而不是一天内构建一百个想法。那会是什么样子？我喜欢说，在我们知道它是正确的产品之前，先把它完全做错。在你确定它正确之前先做错。如果你能直接做对，那很好，但不要因此放慢速度。如果我们一开始就说，'好吧，我今天能相信什么？我相信这是个错误的产品。'你会怎么做不同？你不会浪费三个月去构建错误的产品。我宁愿浪费一天或一周去构建错误的产品。重点是学习。它是否足够好以获取信号并测试我的想法？我们通常可以拆解并用错误的方式测试这些部分。很多时候可能是一个广告。我简直不敢相信有多少团队在推出产品之前甚至没有测试过广告。那是事后才想到的。在 Zynga，这让我震惊。讲一个故事：当我们推出 FarmVille 的第一个扩展包时——这后来成为巨大的业务驱动力——我记得是 FarmVille English Countryside。团队来找我说，'我们有 1000 万美元的广告预算，我们会在发布前开始投放广告。'我说，'等等，你每天有 2500 万到 3000 万用户使用你的产品，但你却要在其他地方投放'即将推出'的广告？你有这些活跃用户。如果你只是在游戏板上放点东西呢？而且，如果你不只是开始产品营销，而是仍然进行产品测试，看看什么最受欢迎呢？如果你开始把不同艺术形式的 English Countryside 锁定在游戏板上，看看有多少点击量呢？开始测试外观、感觉、营销信息。同时，当人们点击时，显示'即将推出。点击这里成为预用户。点击这里提前两周获得访问权限。'结果每个人都点击了，因为那是游戏板上的一个变化。它既为我们指明了正确的营销方向，也指明了产品的正确变体。但意想不到的结果是，我们开始向人们出售'你和朋友'的钥匙，最终卖出了价值 1900 万美元的钥匙，用于提前访问新的扩展包。

transcript.secZh: 创始人模式：扼杀希望与坏点子

turns.en: And so we took something that was going to be afterthought advertising, try to drum up interest in this product and turn it into something that gave us signal and direction on the product and revenues. And so often, you know, you can have an existing product, but you can turn something that was going to be marketing into a kind of scarcity and heat driving feature, something that could drive revenues or something else that matters. So the way we should be using AI is as a testing machine, a failure machine, and a way to vibe code, cloud code, but build the lowest possible cycled version of your product that you can get signal back on.
turns.zh: 所以我们把原本只是事后诸葛的广告，用来激发人们对产品的兴趣，变成了能给我们产品方向和收入信号的东西。很多时候，你可以把一个现有产品中原本用于营销的东西，变成一种稀缺性和热度驱动的功能，从而推动收入或其他重要指标。因此，我们使用 AI 的方式应该是把它当作一个测试机器、一个失败机器，以及一种氛围编码、云端编码的方式，但目标是构建产品的最低可行循环版本，以便从中获得反馈信号。

transcript.secZh: 将营销转化为产品功能

turns.en: Awesome. I want to ask about something that I think might be on people's minds as they hear you talk. For a lot of product builders, when they think Zynga, they're not like this is the product I want to build, you know, and there's a spectrum of products you guys have built. There's like Farmville and Cityville and all these things that I think turn some people off. Then there's like Poker, Words with Friends, Mafia Wars that I think people are like, 'Oh, that's amazing. I love that.' Can you just speak to that?
turns.zh: 太棒了。我想问一个可能很多人听到你说话时会想到的问题。对于很多产品构建者来说，当他们想到 Zynga 时，他们并不会觉得这是他们想构建的产品。你们构建的产品范围很广，有像 Farmville 和 Cityville 这样的，我觉得会让一些人反感。然后还有像 Poker、Words with Friends、Mafia Wars 这样的，我觉得人们会觉得‘哦，太棒了，我喜欢’。你能谈谈这个吗？

turns.en: Sure. I think if I'm unpacking that when you say that the products turn people off, it's funny you say that because Farmville and Cityville were our biggest hits in terms of installs, engagement, retention, revenues. Now Words with Friends and Poker were more enduring because they made the transition to mobile and the other games didn't. That's a whole other story. But I think the part you're getting to is that Zynga was in a lot of ways a victim of its own success. Our games were so viral and took over the feeds on Facebook so much that your friend who was playing it started to bug you as a person not playing it. And that's fair. But what I would say, I think what's misunderstood about Zynga from the beginning and why we succeeded and we created eight massive hits out of 10 mass large game launches. We had a very high hit rate. It wasn't that we were good at virality. It was that we were focused on two things that I think we did better than anybody else. And we had lots of competitors, lots of copying and we still managed to keep winning. So it couldn't be that we were just spammier or better at virality.
turns.zh: 当然。如果我要解读你说的产品让人反感，有趣的是你这么说，因为 Farmville 和 Cityville 在安装量、参与度、留存率和收入方面是我们最大的成功。而 Words with Friends 和 Poker 更持久，因为它们成功过渡到了移动端，其他游戏没有。这是另一个故事了。但我觉得你想说的是，Zynga 在很多方面是自己成功的受害者。我们的游戏病毒式传播太厉害，占据了 Facebook 的信息流，以至于你玩这个游戏的朋友开始打扰不玩的你。这很公平。但我想说的是，我认为从一开始人们对 Zynga 就有误解，我们之所以成功，是因为我们在 10 次大型游戏发布中创造了 8 个巨大成功。我们的成功率很高。不是因为我们擅长病毒式传播，而是因为我们专注于两件事，我认为我们比任何人都做得好。我们有很多竞争对手，很多抄袭，但我们仍然保持胜利。所以不可能仅仅是因为我们更垃圾或更擅长病毒式传播。

transcript.secZh: Zynga 的产品谱系与误解

transcript.sec: Core mission: connecting the world through games

turns.en: First, we had this core mission and focus around connecting the world through games. Gordon and I kept coming back to this idea that I love and I want your founders, product makers watching this to join me in thinking about: how can we add more dimensionality? I love this idea of what I call a bold beat. It's how do I take this thing that's kind of gotten boring, like poker, and add a whole new dimension to it? By adding social, by adding real identities, by giving you this, you're doing social networking, you're in this cocktail party, and now we're giving you a way to actually meet new people or improve your relationships in a game. The reason why so many people, especially middle-aged women, loved Farmville and these games was because they had this kind of hobby, but they weren't doing it alone. They were doing it with their good friends, making new friends. And in the game, our most successful features were co-op play. There were things that let you do gifting and do things of value to your friends. And so we called the games invest, express, and connect. You could do something that made you seen as creative. I like to say people don't want to be creative, they want to feel creative. So we were letting you feel creative, look creative to people. It's what Midjourney does for me today. And then we were letting you express yourself and connect with people around it.
turns.zh: 首先，我们有一个核心使命和焦点，那就是通过游戏连接世界。戈登和我一直回到这个我热爱的想法，我希望正在观看的创始人和产品制造者能和我一起思考：我们如何增加更多的维度？我喜欢这个我称之为‘大胆节拍’的想法。就是如何把一个已经变得无聊的东西，比如扑克，增加一个全新的维度？通过增加社交、真实身份，让你在社交网络中，在鸡尾酒会上，现在我们给你一种在游戏中结识新朋友或改善关系的方式。为什么那么多人，尤其是中年女性，喜欢 Farmville 这些游戏？因为她们有这种爱好，但她们不是独自在做。她们和好朋友一起做，结交新朋友。在游戏中，我们最成功的功能是合作玩法。有让你送礼和为朋友做有价值事情的功能。所以我们把游戏称为投资、表达和连接。你可以做一些让你看起来有创意的事情。我喜欢说人们不想有创意，他们想感觉有创意。所以我们让你感觉有创意，在别人看来有创意。这就是今天 Midjourney 为我做的。然后我们让你表达自己，并围绕它与人连接。

transcript.secZh: 核心使命：通过游戏连接世界

turns.en: Then if you think at a mechanical metric level, our core metric was retention, not virality. We grew. We outlasted everybody because we had the best retention. I think we were the only consumer company in the world that tracked day 365 retention. I don't think anyone tracks it today. I talk about it in the book. I think it's a gift to your founders. I believe that the most valuable companies in the world, statistically this is true, they have the highest day 365 retention. Can you build against day 365 retention? Yes. We can't wait till day 365. We can find early indicators of a positive day 365 and a negative day 365 retention. If you have low D1, low D30, you will probably not have high day 365. So those are correlated. But you can have the opposite: very high day 30 and zero day 365, which is most products. And if you don't think about why someone would use this in a year, if you don't have that mentality, it turns out when someone tries our product, they think that way too. They think, is this worth investing in? Whether it's a game or a camera, am I going to use this in a year? Should I tell my friends about this? Should I bother investing? Should I bother giving it information about me? And I think there's a mentality to that. I like to say viral-based companies, and there have been many, like BeReal, we've seen these, they're sinking speedboats. They're trying to drive faster than they're sinking. They're trying to get more people in. You can either go faster, bail water out faster, which is try to get people back, or plug the hole in the boat, or build a better boat that doesn't have a hole. So we were tracking both in our games and our network: what was the retention? And we were constantly thinking about that.
turns.zh: 然后从机械指标层面来看，我们的核心指标是留存率，而不是病毒式传播。我们成长了。我们比所有人都持久，因为我们有最好的留存率。我认为我们是世界上唯一一家追踪第 365 天留存率的消费者公司。我不认为今天还有人在追踪。我在书里谈到过。我认为这是给创始人的礼物。我相信世界上最有价值的公司，统计上这是真的，它们有最高的第 365 天留存率。你能针对第 365 天留存率来构建吗？是的。我们不能等到第 365 天。我们可以找到第 365 天留存率正面和负面的早期指标。如果你的第 1 天和第 30 天留存率低，你可能不会有高的第 365 天留存率。所以这些是相关的。但反过来也可能：第 30 天留存率很高，但第 365 天留存率为零，这是大多数产品的情况。如果你不考虑为什么有人会在一年后使用这个产品，如果你没有这种心态，结果当别人尝试我们的产品时，他们也会那样想。他们会想，这值得投入吗？无论是游戏还是相机，我一年后还会用吗？我应该告诉朋友吗？我值得投入吗？我值得给它我的信息吗？我认为这是一种心态。我喜欢说基于病毒式传播的公司，有很多，比如 BeReal，我们见过这些，它们是正在下沉的快艇。它们试图开得比下沉速度快。它们试图让更多人进来。你要么开得更快，更快地舀水出去，也就是试图让人们回来，要么堵住船上的洞，要么造一艘没有洞的更好的船。所以我们在游戏和网络中追踪留存率，并不断思考这个问题。

transcript.secZh: 核心指标：留存优于病毒传播

turns.en: I think the two things to take away from Zynga's success in my opinion that I hold is to have a strong innovation vision that translates to what your teams are doing every week. And we even built a metric that's still not used by anyone. This is an Easter egg if anyone doesn't get cut and anyone's still listening. We built a metric called ASN, and it stood for what we measured: what was your active social network. So we looked at how many round trips you had with a friend or another player. Meaning you took a turn and they took a turn back. You gifted them and they gifted you back. And we found that if you went from zero ASN to one, there was an 80% chance we saw you again in the next month. And if we got you to a four, there was an 80% chance we'd see you 22 out of the next 30 days. And so we could actually build and innovate against that metric.
turns.zh: 我认为从 Zynga 的成功中可以学到的两件事是：有一个强大的创新愿景，并将其转化为团队每周的工作。我们甚至建立了一个至今无人使用的指标。这是一个彩蛋，如果这段没被剪掉，还有人听的话。我们建立了一个叫 ASN 的指标，它代表我们测量的东西：你的活跃社交网络。所以我们看你与朋友或其他玩家有多少次往返互动。意思是，你走了一步，他们回了一步。你送了礼物，他们回赠了礼物。我们发现，如果你的 ASN 从零增加到一，那么下个月我们再见到你的概率是 80%。如果我们让你达到四，那么接下来 30 天中有 22 天见到你的概率是 80%。所以我们实际上可以针对这个指标进行构建和创新。

transcript.secZh: 创新指标：活跃社交网络

turns.en: Let's talk about building a consumer social app. Very hard. Very few people have successfully done it and built something durable. We talk about this on the podcast a bunch. It's like nothing works in consumer. Like it works for a little bit and then dies. And you've maybe built more social consumer products than anyone. Like there's like a dozen that have worked. AI obviously changes the game in theory. What do you think needs to be true for someone for something to work again in today's world?
turns.zh: 我们来聊聊打造一款面向消费者的社交应用。这非常难。很少有人成功做到并建立起持久的东西。我们在播客里经常讨论这个。感觉在消费者领域，什么都行不通。就是火一阵子然后就死了。而你可能是打造社交消费产品最多的人。大概有十几个成功案例。理论上，AI 显然会改变游戏规则。你认为在今天的世界里，要想让某个东西再次成功，需要具备什么条件？

turns.en: It's probably equally surprising to you how little we're seeing in social and consumer in general. It seems like people have kind of given up on the idea that a social app could take off or be viral. And I see them every so often. And they've given up for good reason because nothing's proven and nothing's working. So I like to think first is there latent demand? So if just because a category doesn't exist today, it doesn't mean that we don't want it as consumers. In fact, the best opportunities are usually the opposite. And in 2007 when I started Zynga, video gaming was a $23 billion business. But I had stopped playing games. I didn't know anyone who played games. It was like not even a top 10 activity on the web. And I thought there's a latent demand. I love games. I love playing with my family, but I can't get people to play games with me. And it's too much investment. It's too hard. It was crawling across broken glass to get someone to play a game with you. Literally, I got my nephews who are intense gamers to play Rise of Nations with me and we set up two machines for them and two for me and we had FaceTime going on a machine and then the game and we were on a game server and we did that one time and I was like that is a lot of effort and it was fun but we did it once and I said my premise was okay. I believe this could be and should be a mass market activity. I believe adults want to give themselves permission to play, but I got to make it so accessible, so cheap in terms of what it asks for them. Free. Not just free even, which it wasn't. It was $60 to go buy a game. So, I made it free and I said, I'm going to make it a game you already know. Three clicks and you're in. And I'm going to ask five to 15 minutes of you, you know, to do this and you're going to trip over it. It's a breadcrumb somewhere. You're not going to, like I say, we don't go to the comic book store, but if the comics were in the newspaper, we'd read them. So, I believed there was a latent demand. And in fact, today, gaming is a $280 billion industry. And I actually think there's latent demand. Again, I don't play games. No one I know plays games other than my nieces and nephews. And yet it's this big a business. So I actually think there's another massive growth waiting in games because they're this big and it's this boring and adults aren't doing it or they must be some it's very big but none I know. So okay so social and consumer I believe that we have beyond a latent demand for social we are being social right online. We are on Snapchat and Instagram and TikTok, but I believe it's lost the adrenaline. I think a lot about adrenaline. Like, is there a heat in it? Like, are you excited to go get on Instagram or do you feel a little bit like you're eating potato chips? Like, are these positive calories or negative calories or empty calories in the middle? And I think one interesting thing we looked at NPS, net promoter scores, and we saw that when people quit Facebook and quit Instagram, they went from a plus 35 to a negative 35. There's people have a feeling like they just quit smoking. People are proud to tell you they're not on Instagram, right? They're not missing the party. They're like, "Whoa, I got off that." So, I think so. So I think all that to say I think it's the biggest unexplored opportunity there is on the internet. I mean I think I feel very confident that someone is going to reinvent this social experience for the agentic AI age. They're but how are they going to do it? You know, I would guess that they're going to give us productivity again. That they're going part of what we got. People forget Facebook gave us massive social productivity. We were able to stay in the loop with 300 or a thousand friends. LinkedIn gave us massive productivity. They still do, but they started to move away from that productivity into a realm of wasting time because they wanted to engage more and have more ads and less LinkedIn, but they might have ads now, but but definitely, you know, Instagram had TikTok envy. So, you know, they started doing that. And so I think there is a new step function of productivity that we could be getting in that experience that our agents are letting us stay even more in the loop with the people we care about or you know but not wasting our time as much. So and I'll also say this there's a side of this I call this social thing the cocktail party. Okay, that's that's the big instinct vein for me. And I think about that cocktail party and I think where is that happening or where does it want to happen and how how do we add things to the We'd love to host the cocktail party and if we're not, we'd love to be at it. We know it when we see a great cocktail party. You know it. You feel it. You're like, "Oh, I'm so glad I'm here." And it's moves from like obligatory. I'm at this party, it's the birthday party to almost like greed, like I love this party, I'm love the people I'm meeting. And what do you get at a great cocktail party? You often get great leads.
turns.zh: 你可能同样惊讶，我们在社交和消费领域看到的东西太少了。似乎人们已经放弃了社交应用能火起来的想法。我时不时会看到这种情况。他们放弃是有充分理由的，因为没有任何东西被证明有效，也没有任何东西在起作用。所以我首先会想：是否存在潜在需求？仅仅因为某个类别今天不存在，并不意味着我们作为消费者不想要它。事实上，最好的机会往往恰恰相反。2007 年我创办 Zynga 时，电子游戏是一个 230 亿美元的业务。但我已经不玩游戏了。我认识的人里也没人玩游戏。它甚至不是网络上的十大活动之一。我当时认为存在潜在需求。我喜欢游戏。我喜欢和家人一起玩，但我没法让人们和我一起玩游戏。投入太大，太难了。要说服别人和你一起玩游戏，简直像在碎玻璃上爬行。真的，我让我那些狂热的游戏迷侄子们和我一起玩《国家的崛起》，我们给他们装了两台机器，给我装了两台，我们在一台机器上开着 FaceTime，然后玩游戏，我们连上了游戏服务器，就玩了一次。我当时想，这太费劲了，虽然好玩，但只玩了一次。我说我的前提是对的。我相信这可以而且应该成为一种大众市场活动。我相信成年人想让自己有理由去玩，但我必须让它变得非常容易获得，非常便宜，对用户要求很低。免费。甚至不仅仅是免费，因为当时游戏并不免费。买一个游戏要 60 美元。所以我让它免费，我说，我要做一个你已经知道的游戏。点击三次就能进入。我只要求你花 5 到 15 分钟，你会偶然发现它。就像面包屑一样。你不会特意去漫画书店，但如果漫画出现在报纸上，我们就会读。所以我当时相信存在潜在需求。事实上，今天游戏是一个 2800 亿美元的行业。而且我仍然认为存在潜在需求。再次，我不玩游戏。我认识的人除了我的侄女侄子外没人玩游戏。但它却是这么大的生意。所以我实际上认为游戏领域还有另一波巨大增长，因为它已经这么大，却又这么无聊，成年人都不玩，或者它一定很大，但我认识的人都不玩。所以，关于社交和消费，我相信我们不仅有潜在需求，我们实际上已经在网上社交了。我们在用 Snapchat、Instagram 和 TikTok，但我认为它已经失去了那种兴奋感。我经常思考兴奋感。比如，它还有热度吗？你上 Instagram 是感到兴奋，还是有点像在吃薯片？这些是正能量、负能量还是空热量？我认为有趣的一点是，我们看了净推荐值（NPS），发现当人们退出 Facebook 和 Instagram 时，他们的 NPS 从+35 降到了-35。人们有一种刚戒烟的感觉。人们会自豪地告诉你他们不用 Instagram，对吧？他们并不怀念那个派对。他们会说：“哇，我戒掉了。”所以，我认为这是互联网上最大的未被探索的机会。我非常有信心，有人会在智能体式 AI 时代重新发明这种社交体验。但他们要怎么做呢？我猜他们会再次给我们带来生产力。他们会让一部分我们曾经拥有的东西回归。人们忘了 Facebook 给了我们巨大的社交生产力。我们能够与 300 或 1000 个朋友保持联系。LinkedIn 给了我们巨大的生产力。它们现在仍然如此，但它们开始偏离那种生产力，进入浪费时间的领域，因为它们想要更多参与度和更多广告，而 LinkedIn 可能现在也有广告了，但当然，Instagram 嫉妒 TikTok。所以它们开始那样做。因此，我认为我们可以从这种体验中获得一种新的阶梯式生产力提升，我们的智能体让我们能够更好地与关心的人保持联系，同时又不那么浪费时间。我还要说，社交有一个方面，我称之为鸡尾酒会。那对我来说是最大的直觉源泉。我思考那个鸡尾酒会，思考它在哪里发生，或者它想要在哪里发生，以及我们如何添加东西。我们喜欢主持鸡尾酒会，如果我们不主持，我们也喜欢参加。当我们看到一个很棒的鸡尾酒会时，我们就能认出来。你知道它，你感觉到它。你会想：“哦，我真高兴我在这里。”它从一种义务感——我在这个派对上，这是生日派对——变成几乎贪婪的感觉，比如我爱这个派对，我爱我遇到的人。在一个很棒的鸡尾酒会上，你得到了什么？你经常得到很好的线索。

transcript.secZh: 构建消费者社交应用

transcript.sec: Cocktail Party Analogy for Social Reinvention

turns.en: And so that cocktail party that for me it started with Napster when all of a sudden all of us were connected to each other and the great lead was a music file. But then Facebook, LinkedIn, what we were getting was better lead generation. That was better productivity for our time. Before that, we would go to Google for a lead or Craigslist. And it was a lot of noise to signal. And now we're like, "Oh my god, I could get on Facebook and find a date." And it's actually a good lead. I had a good date on Facebook. So what I would say to people is look, if you want to reinvent social, look for where the cocktail is or you could host, and then think about how lead generation is happening. It might sound weird when I say lead generation, but what is LinkedIn? LinkedIn started with lead generation. All the productivity, the value started in the utility of being in this cocktail party today. And by the way, with Zynga, I said, I'm going to go to the cocktail party. Everyone's hanging out on social networks. I'm going to drop games in the middle and give them a new dimension to network in the games because they want to be on Facebook. I want to give them more to do. Today, we're all hanging out on our Claude, on our GPT, but there's no cocktail party. So my Easter egg to people is it's a quiet, lonely cocktail party like the web was before social networking. And my challenge to your listeners is figure out how to make it rowdy, figure out how to make that cocktail party social and socially productive, and you will probably find gold there.
turns.zh: 所以那个鸡尾酒会，对我来说是从 Napster 开始的，当时我们所有人都突然连接在一起，而那个巨大的线索就是音乐文件。但后来 Facebook、LinkedIn，我们得到的是更好的线索生成。那是我们时间上更好的生产力。在那之前，我们会去 Google 或 Craigslist 找线索，但信号噪声比很高。而现在我们就像，‘天哪，我可以在 Facebook 上找到约会对象。’而且这确实是个好线索。我在 Facebook 上有过不错的约会。所以我想对人们说的是，如果你想重塑社交，就去找鸡尾酒会在哪里，或者你可以自己主办，然后思考线索生成是如何发生的。我说‘线索生成’可能听起来很奇怪，但 LinkedIn 是什么？LinkedIn 就是从线索生成开始的。所有的生产力、价值都始于今天这个鸡尾酒会中的实用性。顺便说一句，对于 Zynga，我说，我要去参加鸡尾酒会。每个人都在社交网络上闲逛。我要在中间投放游戏，给他们一个新的维度在游戏中社交，因为他们想待在 Facebook 上。我想给他们更多事情做。今天，我们都泡在我们的 Claude、GPT 上，但没有鸡尾酒会。所以我给人们的彩蛋是，这是一个安静、孤独的鸡尾酒会，就像社交网络之前的网络一样。我给你们听众的挑战是，想办法让它热闹起来，想办法让那个鸡尾酒会变得社交化且具有社交生产力，你很可能会在那里找到金子。

transcript.secZh: 鸡尾酒会类比：社交重塑

transcript.sec: Identifying a B+ Idea vs an A Idea

turns.en: I want to come back to your thread on your question was how do you know when an idea is a B+, right?
turns.zh: 我想回到你的话题，你的问题是，你怎么知道一个想法是 B+，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: I love the question on well, how do you know if it's a B+? And what I would say to you is how do you know if you're with the right person? Like you're dating. What was your experience dating? And my experience has been that when I'm with the right person, I know it. I'm not asking, is this the right person? I'm like yeah. I love this person. This is my person. And that's the best feeling ever. And I hope everyone can experience that. When you're with the A minus person, you're with the B+. You're asking, could this be the one? You're like, you're and you know, Osho said if you're torn between two paths, they're both wrong. You know, you have feet in two canoes. If you're asking whether or not your product is an A, it's not an A. And you're full of hope. You hope it's an A. It doesn't mean you might not have the chance to turn that into an A, but the first point of intellectual honesty is to say, okay, it's not. And then, well, how do you know how are you getting validation that it's not? You don't have the validation that it is. When you have lightning in a bottle, when you have true signal, everything works. It's anecdotally, you love your product. You're addicted to it. You show it to friends and they love it. Your metrics show that it works. I mean, did we have to ask if GPT was it? Did we say, "Oh, is GPT it? I'm not sure." You know, we're like, "No, I'm living on that and it's making me dream of new things to do." Right? So, I'd say the hard part about this is, you know, what do you do with a B+? First, can we be intellectually honest that it is a B+? And then what do we do with a B+? Do we just kill it and start all over again? Do we use it to learn? The power is knowing it's a B+. That's the first point is okay, this is not it. Now, what do I do with that? Is it a way to learn? Do I see examples that are near this that are it? Can I find anything proven or near proven? When we realize it's not it, we look for more things to do and it's such a hard lesson. Two weeks ago, I pulled the plug on my Earth project for like the fourth time. Okay, I've been building my version of the metaverse for 20 years. It's what led me to Zynga. I call the metaverse that I want life at the speed of play. We're getting closer and closer to that anyway with AI. And by the way, I define the metaverse, I give Reed Hoffman credit for this definition, as blurring the lines between the virtual and the real. And that is where our lives are going anyway. So we're getting more and more metaversian. It's not being in this other escape to immersive world. It's that the virtual and the real completely blur together. But I kept coming at it from the game side and I went native, not in the good way. You know, I got so into wanting to prove I could build this experience that I was building this game engine, web browser-based game engine. And I was not getting product-market fit. I was being too ambitious. I was not going for a small idea. I was going for a really big idea. And it was really hard and I pulled the plug on it after four years in and $25 million in just this one version of it. turns.zh: 我喜欢这个问题，嗯，你怎么知道它是 B+？我想对你说的是，你怎么知道你和对的人在一起？就像你在约会。你约会的经历是什么？我的经验是，当我和对的人在一起时，我知道。我不会问‘这是对的人吗？’而是‘是的，我爱这个人。这就是我要找的人。’那是最棒的感觉。我希望每个人都能体验到。当你和一个 A- 的人在一起时，你实际上是在和一个 B+ 的人在一起。你在问‘这会是那个对的人吗？’你就像，你知道，奥修说过，如果你在两条路之间犹豫不决，那么两条路都是错的。你知道，你两只脚各踩一条船。如果你在问你的产品是不是 A，那它就不是 A。你充满了希望。你希望它是 A。这并不意味着你没有机会把它变成 A，但第一个智力上的诚实是承认，好吧，它不是。然后，嗯，你怎么知道？你怎么得到它不是的验证？你没有它是的验证。当你拥有瓶中的闪电，当你拥有真正的信号时，一切都会奏效。从轶事上讲，你热爱你的产品。你沉迷于它。你把它展示给朋友，他们也喜欢它。你的指标显示它有效。我的意思是，我们需要问 GPT 是不是那个东西吗？我们说过‘哦，GPT 是那个东西吗？我不确定’吗？你知道，我们就像‘不，我靠它生活，它让我梦想做新的事情。’对吧？所以，我想说困难的部分是，你知道，你拿一个 B+ 怎么办？首先，我们能不能在智力上诚实，承认它是 B+？然后我们拿 B+ 怎么办？我们直接扼杀它，重新开始？还是用它来学习？力量在于知道它是 B+。这是第一点：好吧，这不是那个东西。现在，我拿它怎么办？它是一种学习的方式吗？我有没有看到接近这个的例子是那个东西？我能不能找到任何被证明或接近被证明的东西？当我们意识到它不是那个东西时，我们寻找更多的事情去做，这是一个非常艰难的教训。两周前，我第四次终止了我的地球项目。好吧，我建造我版本的元宇宙已经 20 年了。正是它引领我创立了 Zynga。我把我想象的元宇宙称为‘游戏速度的生活’。无论如何，随着 AI 的发展，我们正越来越接近那个目标。顺便说一句，我定义元宇宙，我归功于里德·霍夫曼的这个定义，是模糊虚拟与现实的界限。而我们的生活方式无论如何都在朝那个方向发展。所以我们变得越来越‘元宇宙化’。它不是进入另一个逃避现实的沉浸式世界。而是虚拟和现实完全融合在一起。但我一直从游戏的角度切入，我变得‘原生’了，但不是好的那种。你知道，我太想证明我能构建这种体验，以至于我在构建一个游戏引擎，一个基于浏览器的游戏引擎。但我没有找到产品市场契合。我太雄心勃勃了。我没有追求一个小想法。我追求的是一个非常大的想法。这非常困难，我在投入四年时间和 2500 万美元在这个版本上之后终止了它。而在那之后的两个星期里，我比过去四年受到了更多灵感的启发。所以，终止一个 B+ 项目是有力量的，甚至更多，你知道，终止一个 D 项目。

transcript.secZh: AI 时代的分发挑战

turns.en: It could be. I hope it becomes one, but it's not yet. And that we're kind of like halfway to a platform. We are in a point of discovery. New technologies that break through at the consumer level unlock discovery. That's really important. Discovery means when we first got our phones, we were installing new apps all the time. Now we don't ever install new apps. You know, the average app installs per user per month is zero. So, it's really important to think about that not only are we not in discovery, but even when we are, it doesn't stick. You know, there were something like 40,000 new games launched last year in the app store and zero became top 10 hits and zero even sustained top 25 or 50. So, your odds are bad. So it's not an ideal moment to be launching consumer with high confidence. And that's just something that we have to acknowledge. And it makes consumer categories like social and games almost not investable. They're still getting investment, but it's very hard. It's much easier to be prosumer or enterprise now. For good reason because we're seeing those companies scale revenue ARRs very quickly. So I do think it's a great time to be working on consumer ideas, and you asked about distribution. Distribution is just so crucial and core to any consumer business idea. And it can't be an afterthought. It's not 'I'm going to build the best mousetrap and they're going to come.' Distribution has to be part of your product and baked into the strategy deeply and proven from the beginning. And if you're just building this product and hoping they will come, hoping it'll spread virally or word of mouth, that's a hope strategy, not a belief strategy. And it doesn't mean you shouldn't keep experimenting. Now is a great time to experiment. In fact, it'll be a hundred times more expensive to try to get the same things in the market in a year because there'll be a hundred times more noise. And once there is distribution, there'll be a flood of competition. But when distribution is this broken, it is even more core to your product and your strategy. I think we're seeing many more successful companies that find a prosumer approach, that go after their power users, their whales, the people who care enough to find it, care enough to spend money on it upfront to sustain a business long before we can get out to consumer.
turns.zh: 有可能。我希望它成为平台，但还不是。我们大概处于通往平台的半路上。我们正处于一个发现期。在消费层面取得突破的新技术会解锁发现，这非常重要。发现意味着，当我们刚拿到手机时，我们一直在安装新应用。现在，我们几乎从不安装新应用。平均每个用户每月安装的应用数为零。所以，很重要的一点是，我们不仅不在发现期，而且即使有发现，也无法持久。去年应用商店发布了大约 4 万款新游戏，但没有一款进入前十，甚至没有一款能维持在前 25 或前 50。所以你的胜算很低。因此，现在不是高信心推出消费产品的理想时机。我们必须承认这一点。这使得社交和游戏等消费类别几乎不值得投资。它们仍然有投资，但非常困难。现在做专业消费者或企业级产品要容易得多。这是有原因的，因为我们看到这些公司的 ARR（年经常性收入）增长非常快。所以我确实认为现在是做消费创意的好时机，你问到了分销。分销对任何消费商业创意都至关重要，是核心。它不能是事后才想到的。不是‘我造出最好的捕鼠器，他们就会来’。分销必须成为你产品的一部分，深深融入战略，并从一开始就得到验证。如果你只是构建产品，希望他们会来，希望它会病毒式传播或口口相传，那是希望策略，不是信念策略。这并不意味着你不应该继续实验。现在正是实验的好时机。事实上，一年后试图在市场上获得同样的东西，成本会高出一百倍，因为噪音会多一百倍。一旦有了分销，竞争就会蜂拥而至。但当分销如此破碎时，它对你的产品和战略就更加核心。我们看到很多成功的公司采用专业消费者方法，瞄准他们的重度用户、大客户，那些足够关心、愿意提前花钱来维持业务的人，远在我们能触及大众消费者之前。

turns.en: There is a great idea that Gary Tan had that I'll repeat here because I loved it so much this week: if we get in the mindset of basically intelligence on tap, free tokens, and if we think that the amount of tokens we're buying today will basically be free in a year or two, that the token supply is going to go up so much. Our demand will too, but if today we start building re-imagining consumer services based on free tokens, I think that's such an interesting innovation zone. It could sound like a dot-com business plan. I remember ordering pig ears for my dog Zynga and wanting to send thank you notes to the VCs because they were cheaper than at Petco, right? That's not a good business plan. So giving away tokens at a loss isn't a good business plan, but it might be a phenomenal business because what the dot-com thing had wrong was the price of pig ears wasn't going to come down or delivery wasn't going to come down, but the price of tokens will. So I think that's another kind of Easter egg of an insight that I'm intrigued by, and I think founders are going to do something really interesting with it. Because games don't work, premium games and premium apps that have to go and hit the AI, even if you wanted to spend on it, you're hitting token limits.
