I want to go in a different direction and touch on all these really amazing nuggets of advice for scaling a company, managing people, hiring people, working with people. One is this idea of making everybody a CEO at your company. Talk about that lesson.
turns.zh: 是的，那会很有趣。我们过去有微软的例子。微软是一个残酷的垄断者，一个残酷的平台。他们吞噬了周围的一切。然后在我们这里，我们在 Facebook 上也看到了。他们不关心的两个领域是音乐，因为知识产权问题很麻烦，以及游戏，因为他们不想雇佣艺术家。其他一切他们都吸收了。我想换个方向，谈谈你那些关于扩大公司规模、管理人才、招聘和与人合作的精彩建议。其中一个是让公司里的每个人都成为 CEO。谈谈这个经验。

turns.en: All my management principles I got through desperation. I don't like managing people. I say every day you're managing people is a day of work. It's a necessary evil for us as product makers because we can't abdicate and just have someone else be CEO, because all of a sudden the priorities get messed up, teams don't have to listen to us, and we're now feedback, not direction. That's a failing path. So we have to be a CEO. But then the question is how do we get to spend the most time doing what we love and the least time doing what we don't? The bigger principle I started to get to was that all of management is just how do we get people to do the right thing when we're not in the room. So I was like, okay, if I give them a hill to take, and if I make them a CEO, make them a real CEO—they have operating control, degrees of freedom to take that hill however they want, and they can give me a plan, a budget, and then do whatever they want within that. What I found was I didn't have to manage them. They're not coming back to me with questions. Certain kinds of people really want to be a CEO. What I also found was it was incredibly motivating for people. It's motivating to me. I always say, I'm a team player as long as I'm running the team. So can I hire other people like that? I like to say I was a frustrated expert witness when I worked for other people. Those are the best people to turn into a CEO. They're a little bit of a know-it-all, they think they have the right answers, and we're going to find out if they do. So we let them actually be a CEO, and they have all this pent-up demand to go and prove that they were right. That's a beautiful thing. So I love this principle of make everyone a CEO. We're kind of going there in Silicon Valley with this idea of getting rid of middle management. Brian Armstrong was saying everyone should be an individual contributor. Everyone should be managing a lot of other people. That's really to me the best CEO is like the best player at the position, doing the thing they're great at, and not wasting their time on management.
turns.zh: 我所有的管理原则都是出于绝望。我不喜欢管理人。我说，你管理人一天，就是工作了一天。作为产品制造者，这是必要的恶，因为我们不能放弃，让别人当 CEO，因为那样优先级会乱，团队不必听我们的，我们变成了反馈而不是方向。那是一条失败的路。所以我们必须是 CEO。但问题是如何让我们花最多时间做我们喜欢的事，最少时间做我们不喜欢的事？我开始领悟到的更大原则是，所有管理就是如何让人们在不在场时做正确的事。所以我想，好吧，如果我给他们一座山去攻占，如果我让他们成为 CEO，真正的 CEO——他们有运营控制权，有自由度以他们想要的方式攻占那座山，他们可以给我一个计划、一个预算，然后在那范围内做任何事。我发现我不需要管理他们。他们不会回来问我问题。某些人真的想当 CEO。我还发现这对人们非常有激励作用。这对我也有激励作用。我总是说，只要我领导团队，我就是团队的一员。那么我能雇佣其他像这样的人吗？我喜欢说，当我为别人工作时，我是一个受挫的专家证人。那些人是成为 CEO 的最佳人选。他们有点自以为是，认为他们有正确答案，我们会看看他们是否真的有。所以我们让他们真正成为 CEO，他们积压了所有需求去证明他们是对的。那是一件美好的事。所以我喜欢这个让每个人都成为 CEO 的原则。我们在硅谷正朝着这个方向走，即取消中层管理。Brian Armstrong 说每个人都应该是个体贡献者。每个人都应该管理很多人。对我来说，最好的 CEO 就像最好的球员，做他们擅长的事，不把时间浪费在管理上。

turns.en: You mentioned this idea of an expert witness and you also have this nugget of advice of staying close to the metal. I love this general advice. Talk about what you mean there, why that's important for someone in their career.
turns.zh: 你提到了专家证人的概念，你还有一个建议是贴近底层。我喜欢这个通用建议。谈谈你是什么意思，为什么这对一个人的职业生涯很重要。

turns.en: They go together. We start our careers in our 20s very close to the metal, meaning we are individual contributors and we are working on getting the primary data and building the actual product. Usually we're in the trenches, and the people in the trenches are usually closest to the data, closest to probably the right answer, but they're also usually furthest from making the decision. That's what I call the expert witness syndrome, because you're called to the stand by the adults and you plead for what you passionately believe is the right answer, and then you're excused, and then they make a decision, and then you have to deal with the consequences, and you know, disagree and commit. What if I'm disagreeing because I'm right, and now I'm committing to clean up your mess because you're wrong?
turns.zh: 它们是相辅相成的。我们在二十多岁时开始职业生涯，非常贴近底层，意思是我们是个人贡献者，致力于获取一手数据并构建实际产品。通常我们在战壕里，战壕里的人通常最接近数据，最接近正确答案，但他们也通常最远离决策。这就是我所说的专家证人综合症，因为你被“成年人”叫到证人席，你为你深信不疑的正确答案辩护，然后你被解雇，他们做出决定，然后你必须处理后果，你知道，不同意但承诺。如果我不同意是因为我是对的，而现在我承诺要收拾你的烂摊子，因为你错了，那该怎么办？

transcript.secZh: 消费者代理服务

transcript.sec: Founders stay close to the metal

turns.en: And that's why many of us become founders, because we're just sick of being in that position. You're also closer to the metal. And when you become a CEO, when you become a founder, part of the paradox that I believe we have to be careful about is that we stay close to the metal. And I believe the best product CEOs are in the minutiae of the details. You know, all the stories about Steve Jobs. I was so impressed when I heard that he insisted on picking out the carpeting in the conference rooms. And I tried to have that same mentality of micromanaging the really small pixel-level details that matter. And I had a great conversation with the founders of Discord, and they came to this conclusion too, that they needed to actually be an inverted pyramid. And they said, "We realized that we outsource, we delegate the most important product decisions, the UX, to our least experienced people, and they're doing this every day." And we decided to turn that upside down and say, "We as founders need to be kind of the first and last mile for the product." And we have to be the ones who bless it. Our best use of our time is making these minute decisions that change the product user experience. And so to me, that's being close to the metal. Brian Chesky believes in doing things in non-scalable ways and doing it himself with the team by hand. There are so many stories about Jeff Bezos and Zuckerberg of the thing that mattered most: they would go and work two days a week deeply with the team on it. And it makes sense. If you're the best product maker in the company, we want you on the field. We want to put you on the ice. We don't want you to spend your time talking to investors and managing and scaling.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我们很多人成为创始人，因为我们厌倦了那种位置。你也更贴近一线。当你成为 CEO、成为创始人时，我认为我们必须小心一个悖论：保持贴近一线。我相信最好的产品 CEO 都沉浸在细节中。你知道所有关于史蒂夫·乔布斯的故事。当我听说他坚持亲自挑选会议室的毯子时，我印象非常深刻。我也试图拥有那种心态，去微观管理那些真正重要的像素级细节。我和 Discord 的创始人有过一次很棒的对话，他们也得出了同样的结论：他们需要成为一个倒金字塔。他们说：‘我们意识到，我们把最重要的产品决策——用户体验——外包给了最缺乏经验的人，而他们每天都在做这些。’我们决定把它颠倒过来，说：‘作为创始人，我们需要成为产品的第一英里和最后一英里。’我们必须是那个拍板的人。我们最好的时间利用方式就是做出这些改变产品用户体验的微小决策。所以对我来说，这就是贴近一线。布莱恩·切斯基相信用不可扩展的方式做事，亲自和团队一起手工完成。有很多关于杰夫·贝佐斯和扎克伯格的故事，他们最看重的事情就是每周花两天时间深入团队一起工作。这很有道理。如果你是公司里最好的产品制造者，我们希望你在场上。我们希望把你放在冰面上。我们不希望你花时间跟投资者交谈、管理团队和扩张规模。

turns.en: Yeah. Elon is famous for this too. You have this line in the book along these lines: micromanagement is beautiful. You should micromanage as long as you can.
turns.zh: 是的。埃隆也以这点闻名。你在书里也有类似的说法：微观管理是美好的。你应该尽可能长时间地进行微观管理。

turns.en: I believe that, and I did that building Zynga to a fault. I mean, we got to a point that I had all the way to 50 employees, we had a standup call that would go on for two hours, and I had everyone's name in a spreadsheet, and I had what they were supposed to do yesterday and what they're going to do today, and I go through name by name and say, "Okay, did you get that done? Can everyone else verify they got that done? Okay, what are you going to do today?" And I completely micromanaged, but we were much more effective. And then I kept micromanaging things as I'm like, if you can be in the room, be in the room assuming that you are the best player. So yeah, I think it's less contrarian saying it now than it was 20 years ago. But when I was building Zynga, we had to apologize for things like micromanaging or expecting massive results from our teams. And I think I'm happy to see that that's becoming kind of accepted as closer to best practices today.
turns.zh: 我相信这一点，我在创建 Zynga 时也这样做了，甚至到了过分的程度。我的意思是，当我们有 50 名员工时，我们有一个持续两小时的站会，我把每个人的名字都放在一个电子表格里，记录他们昨天应该做什么、今天要做什么，然后我一个一个地问：‘好了，你完成了吗？其他人能确认他完成了吗？好的，你今天要做什么？’我完全进行了微观管理，但我们的效率高得多。然后我一直保持微观管理，因为我觉得，如果你能待在房间里，就待在房间里，假设你是最好的玩家。所以，是的，我认为现在说这个不像 20 年前那么反主流了。但在我创建 Zynga 的时候，我们不得不为微观管理或期望团队取得巨大成果之类的事情道歉。我很高兴看到这现在越来越被接受，更接近最佳实践了。

turns.en: But my point about management is to get people to do the right thing when you're not in the room. The first principle is: be in the room if you can be, as much as you can be, and then only delegate when you get to the point that you can't possibly be in all these rooms at the same time. And then all these management principles are just different strategies to try to get people to do the right thing when you're not there. And I love these non-scalable ideas that actually scale what you're trying to do as a product maker in the right way. And one of the ones I love is this idea of tech assistants and this idea of a teaching hospital. How do you pass the vampire blood of you to other people? Like you have this burning mission and passion around your product. How do you spread that to other people and have them spread it in the organization? The first thing is put them in the room with you. So the idea of this teaching hospital was: okay, I'm just going to put as many people as I can in the room while I'm doing these product management meetings and have my passion and ideas and approach spread to all of them too, and that worked. And then grab someone from the ranks and make them your tech assistant. Have them just follow you around for six months or 12 months and make them the mini-me, the person who is you in your 20s who was the expert witness, a little bit of a know-it-all. And start having them follow you around to pick up, learn, absorb everything you do, and give them projects to test them. And then hopefully you've created a mini-me that you can now put in a much bigger role somewhere else. And in fact, Andy Jassy at Amazon, everyone on the S-team used to be someone who had been the tech assistant to Bezos. So that idea actually scales, and you kind of get it for free.
turns.zh: 但我关于管理的观点是：让人们在你不场时做正确的事。首要原则是：尽可能待在房间里，只有当你无法同时出现在所有房间时才去授权。然后所有这些管理原则只是不同的策略，试图让人们在你不场时做正确的事。我喜欢这些不可扩展的想法，它们实际上以正确的方式扩展了你作为产品制造者想做的事情。其中一个我喜欢的想法是技术助理和教学医院的概念。你如何把你的‘吸血鬼之血’传递给其他人？就像你对产品有燃烧的使命和激情，你如何把它传播给其他人，让他们在组织中传播？首先，让他们和你一起待在房间里。所以教学医院的想法是：好吧，我在做产品管理会议时，尽可能多地把人带进房间，让我的激情、想法和方法也传播给他们，这很有效。然后从团队中抓一个人，让他做你的技术助理。让他跟着你六个月或十二个月，让他成为你的迷你版，那个二十多岁时的你，那个专家证人，有点万事通的样子。然后让他跟着你，学习、吸收你所做的一切，并给他项目来测试他。然后希望你已经创造了一个迷你版，现在可以把他放在其他地方一个更大的角色上。事实上，亚马逊的安迪·贾西，S-team 的每个人都曾是贝佐斯的技术助理。所以这个想法实际上是可以扩展的，而且你几乎是免费得到了它。

turns.en: I love this phrase of transferring your vampire blood. Oh man. Maybe one last nugget from the book along these lines is you have this line: just the number one job of a CEO is to be right. Talk about that.
turns.zh: 我喜欢‘传递你的吸血鬼之血’这个说法。天哪。也许这本书里还有一个与此相关的金句：CEO 的第一要务就是正确。谈谈这个吧。

turns.en: Well, that's another one that I stole from Bezos. But if I got to pick one thing that you do as CEO, I'm going to pick that you're right. So even if you don't operate the ship or the factory that well, I'd rather that you picked the right product, the right strategy, than you were phenomenal at execution or inspiring people or managing. Because being in the right body of water matters more than the right boat. And a great boat in a dead lake bed isn't going anywhere. And so I look for that in people: are they right? That's the best resume. I could see that you were right about something. And I look for that in the team. And it's less do I like your style or approach or personality fit. I'm like, I'll take misfits who are right. I want those really smart expert witnesses who are intellectually honest. And the more people you have around you who are right, they're putting balls in the net.
turns.zh: 嗯，这是我从贝佐斯那里偷来的另一个观点。但如果让我选择 CEO 做的一件事，我会选择‘正确’。所以即使你运营船或工厂不那么好，我宁愿你选择了正确的产品、正确的战略，而不是你在执行、激励或管理方面非常出色。因为身处正确的水域比拥有正确的船更重要。一艘好船在干涸的湖床上哪儿也去不了。所以我寻找人们身上的这一点：他们正确吗？那是最好的简历。我能看到你在某件事上是正确的。我在团队中也寻找这一点。我不太在意你的风格、方法或个性是否合适。我会接受那些正确但不合群的人。我想要那些真正聪明、知识诚实的专家证人。你身边正确的人越多，他们就越能进球得分。

turns.en: I want to maybe end with a question about your kids and parenting. I talked to a bunch of people that know you well in preparation for this conversation, and every single person commented on just how wonderful a dad you are and how much time you put into that part of your life. And a question I've been trying to ask folks that are parents and great parents in this AI era. There's a lot of stuff I want to ask about, but let me start here: what's something that you are teaching them or helping them to develop that you think is going to be important to them in this crazy world we're entering of AI?
turns.zh: 我想也许以一个关于你的孩子和育儿的问题来结束。在准备这次对话时，我和很多了解你的人聊过，每个人都评论说你是一位多么出色的父亲，你在生活的这一部分投入了多少时间。我一直想向那些在这个 AI 时代为人父母、而且是优秀父母的人问一个问题。有很多东西我想问，但让我从这里开始：你在教他们什么，或者帮助他们发展什么，你认为在这个我们正在进入的疯狂 AI 世界里，这对他们很重要？

turns.en: Thank you for asking about this. I think it's my greatest role and job and service to the world is trying to grow good humans.
turns.zh: 谢谢你问这个。我认为我最大的角色、工作和对世界的贡献就是努力培养善良的人。

transcript.secZh: 创始人贴近一线

transcript.sec: Parenting Principles and Meeting Kids Where They Are

turns.en: So I really put a lot into it and take it seriously, and I get so much out of it. I like to say I'm growing my best friends, and so far it's true. There are a couple principles and philosophies that have been important to me as a parent. I do them naturally and I see they work over time. The first one for me is that I have five kids, and every one of them is such a different version of a human. I have a special needs son who's fairly extreme, and our new one-year-old baby has a gene mutation and may end up being very neurodivergent. So you have this wide range. My 15-year-old girls are twins and completely divergent from each other. This first principle I've found is meeting them where they are. With kids, we can kind of talk down to them or treat them like adults, and neither is right. We have to engage with them at the altitude they're at, but as human to human, and find a way to do that. Once we meet them where they are, we can bring them to surprisingly sophisticated places that may be way beyond where they're supposed to be at that moment. For example, my twins—I started playing with math with them when they were really little. During the pandemic, I taught 'daddy math' because I saw their school wasn't equipped for online homeschooling. I taught them and their friends daddy math, just trying to teach them a math brain. I didn't know where they were supposed to be—they were in fifth grade—but I just started trying to teach them how to approach math in a fun and curious way. Later we found out I taught them all the way through eighth grade math, and I didn't know it and they didn't know it. That was cool.
turns.zh: 所以我真的投入了很多，认真对待，也从中收获了很多。我喜欢说我在培养自己最好的朋友，到目前为止确实如此。有几个原则和理念对我作为家长来说很重要。我自然而然地践行它们，并且看到它们随着时间的推移是有效的。第一个原则是，我有五个孩子，每个都是截然不同的个体。我有一个特殊需求的儿子，情况相当严重；我们一岁大的宝宝有基因突变，可能也会是神经多样性儿童。所以跨度很大。我 15 岁的女儿们是双胞胎，但性格完全不同。我发现第一个原则就是接纳他们本来的样子。对待孩子，我们可能会居高临下地跟他们说话，或者把他们当大人对待，但这两种都不对。我们必须以他们所处的水平与他们互动，但要以人对人的方式，找到合适的方法。一旦我们接纳了他们本来的样子，就能把他们带到令人惊讶的成熟境地，可能远超他们那个年龄应有的水平。比如我的双胞胎女儿——她们很小的时候我就开始和她们玩数学。疫情期间，我教她们“爸爸数学”，因为我觉得她们的学校不具备在线教学的能力。我教她们和她们的朋友爸爸数学，只是想培养她们的数学思维。我不知道她们应该学到什么程度——她们当时五年级——但我只是开始教她们如何用有趣和好奇的方式对待数学。后来我们发现，我实际上教完了八年级的数学，而我和她们当时都不知道。这很酷。

transcript.secZh: 育儿原则与因材施教

transcript.sec: Education in the Age of AI and Critical Thinking

turns.en: In terms of how this applies in the age of AI, I feel like we're at the end of a hundred-year cycle of mass-produced education, which was about getting the most knowledge in, raising everyone's averages, educating 20 million graduates a year or whatever number. It was factory-produced, first for factories, then for knowledge workers. Knowledge working is going away—it's changing—but we're still teaching knowledge education. I feel badly about it with my kids. What I've tried to do with my older kids is teach them critical thinking more than anything. I say, 'I don't care if you go to college,' which is a little hard for them because they're achievers. I care that you develop critical thinking and find a way to be useful to people in the world. My daughter Carmen, she's also neurodivergent—she has ADHD and dyslexia—and she created a brand of sweatshirts called Comfy Fancy, and also a group for neurodivergent middle school kids called Neurosparkley. She's taking something that could be a problem or deficit and making it a way to connect and help other kids. I'm proud to see that. The dissonance I feel as a parent is that my kids are in an educational system trying to prep them for a good college and to be knowledge workers, but I know that game is over. So what do I want my kids doing? I try to teach them to look at the deeper meta of what's going on. When an adult is trying to convince you of something, when a teacher has a point of view you don't agree with, try to understand why. What's their agenda? Where are they coming from? What's their lived experience? I'm trying to teach them to ask better questions, not know more answers.
turns.zh: 谈到这在 AI 时代如何应用，我觉得我们正处于一个百年周期的大规模生产式教育的末期，这种教育旨在灌输最多的知识，提高平均分，每年培养 2000 万毕业生之类的。它是工厂式的，最初为工厂服务，后来为知识工作者服务。知识工作正在消失——它在变化——但我们仍在教授知识教育。我为我的孩子感到遗憾。我对大一点的孩子所做的，就是教他们批判性思维，而不是别的。我说：‘我不在乎你们是否上大学’，这对他们来说有点难，因为他们是有成就欲的人。我在乎的是你们培养批判性思维，并找到一种方式对世界上的其他人有用。我的女儿卡门，她也是神经多样性——她有 ADHD 和阅读障碍——她创立了一个叫 Comfy Fancy 的运动衫品牌，还成立了一个针对神经多样性初中生的团体叫 Neurosparkley。她把可能成为问题或缺陷的东西，变成了连接和帮助其他孩子的方式。我很自豪看到这一点。我作为家长感到的失调是，我的孩子身处一个试图让他们为上好大学和成为知识工作者做准备的教育体系，但我知道那套游戏已经结束了。那么我希望我的孩子做什么呢？我试图教他们看清事情背后的深层本质。当一个成年人试图说服你某事，当一个老师持有你不同意的观点时，试着理解为什么。他们的动机是什么？他们从何而来？他们的生活经历是怎样的？我试图教他们提出更好的问题，而不是知道更多的答案。

transcript.secZh: AI 时代的教育与批判性思维

turns.en: In terms of how that spills into their online use and what I try to promote and discourage, I try to promote how can you be generative in what you're doing and not consumptive. So what can you do online or offline that is generative—actually putting something new out in the world—because that's how you're going to get something back, not consuming, not being a passive consumer of content and experiences. Sometimes it works—I see them doing that—and sometimes it doesn't. That's how I'm trying to navigate this. I also tried to make it till they were 16 with no phones, no smartphones, and I got to 14. I got them flip phones, and they had internships at Niantic working on Pokémon Go. They built a first-time user experience—it was really good. I think they used smartphones for that, and they still have them. So even though I tried to make it, I didn't.
turns.zh: 至于这如何延伸到他们的网络使用，以及我试图提倡和劝阻什么，我提倡的是如何在你所做的事情中具有创造性，而不是消费性。所以你在线上或线下能做什么是创造性的——真正向世界输出新东西——因为这样你才能得到回报，而不是消费，不是被动地消费内容和体验。有时这奏效——我看到他们这样做——有时则不。这就是我试图应对的方式。我还试图让他们到 16 岁都没有手机，没有智能手机，但我坚持到了 14 岁。我给他们买了翻盖手机，他们在 Niantic 实习，参与 Pokémon Go 的工作。他们构建了首次用户体验——做得非常好。我想他们为此用了智能手机，现在也还在用。所以尽管我努力了，但没完全做到。

transcript.secZh: 推广技术的生成式使用

turns.en: For my older girls, I keep a running Google doc of life philosophies and anything I say to them as a quote I say over and over. I'm like, 'Okay, I'm going to write that up with stories and keep it in this document for them.' Who knows if they'll use it, but they do start to repeat some of them, so I know they read it. One is 'Nothing's personal.' Don't ever take anything personally. If you assume nothing's personal, you're probably right 19 out of 20 times. And the 20th time, you probably handled it right because of that. Another is 'Don't be a victim.' The world doesn't happen to you; it's happening around you. We're defined by how we react to the world, not by the events that happen to us. So I'm trying to give them these. They're teenagers with the older two, and I'm in it and learning. I've got two little ones—a one-year-old and a four-year-old—who sleep with me every night.
turns.zh: 对于我大一点的女儿们，我保持着一个不断更新的谷歌文档，记录人生哲学以及我对她们反复说过的话。我会说：‘好吧，我要把这个写下来，配上故事，保存在这个文档里给她们。’谁知道她们会不会用，但她们确实开始重复其中一些，所以我知道她们读了。其中一条是‘没什么是针对你的’。永远不要觉得事情是针对你的。如果你假设没什么是针对你的，那么 20 次里有 19 次你可能是对的。而第 20 次，正因为这个假设，你可能也处理对了。另一条是‘不要做受害者’。世界不是发生在你身上，而是发生在你周围。我们是由我们对世界的反应来定义的，而不是由发生在我们身上的事件。所以我试图把这些教给她们。最大的两个是青少年，我身处其中，也在学习。我还有两个小的——一个一岁，一个四岁——每晚都和我一起睡。
